text: I'd like to thank you all for coming to the session on complications. Whoops, what do I do now? This session is chaired by Amy Neville, who is a minimally invasive surgeon from Ottawa, and myself. My name is John Hagan, and I work at Humber River Hospital in Toronto. And during the course of surgery, we are all faced with different options, and often there's no right way to manage a complication when it occurs. But as surgeons, we are trained to manage when things don't go totally as planned. However, sometimes it's helpful to have input from other surgeons as to what might be some options as to how to get out of a very tight situation. Thanks, John. So I'll just introduce the format a little bit. What we want is for this to be quite informal. We basically want our panelists to imagine they're sitting in the lounge waiting for the next case or in their office and they're called by a colleague for help. We will use some kind of multiple choice questions to help the discussion, but you're certainly free to answer as you feel fit. We do expect some disagreement among our panelists, potentially among audience members, as these are not straightforward cases and there certainly is no unique right answer for each one. We're hoping to show five cases, possibly four, depending on how much time and how much discuss and we generate. And I would like to also thank our panelists for being present. So we have Dr. Michael Brunt from Washington University School of Medicine, Dr. Melissa Lipinska from University of Tennessee, and Dr. Ted Truss from Dartmouth Medical Center in New Hampshire. Dr. Ellie may also be joining us as well. So I'll let John lead the first case.

Okay, this is our first case, and I'll give you a bit of a case history. You can ask some questions at the end of the case after we're finished talking about it. And so this first case, a thirty-seven year old woman, she's got a small umbilical hernia, and she's pretty skinny, like her BMI is about twenty, and she's got a symptomatic right inguinal hernia. So my question to the panel to start with is, is she suitable for a laparoscopic inguinal repair? So maybe Michael, did you do laparoscopic hernia repairs? Yes, I do lap inguinals. I do a lot of open inguinals as well. I think this patient could be managed with either approach. The advantages of an open repair in a woman, or you can do it under, or a man for that matter, you can do it under local anesthesia with sedation. You could potentially, in a woman, do a primary sutured repair as opposed to a mesh repair. Now, the problem with an open mesh repair in a woman is that there's not a big cord there to protect the nerves, so you may be a little vulnerable for lingering groin pain afterwards. But I think a laparoscopic approach is a perfectly reasonable option. You want to be careful not to use too much mesh fixation. Most of these are indirect, and the other point is that that sac tends to be really, really intimately associated with the round ligament, so it can be difficult to separate from it.

Melissa, do you do laparoscopic hernia repairs? I do. I'd say that my practice is probably about eighty percent laparoscopic and I would tend to say that, you know, thinking about it from myself as a thirty-seven-year-old lady, I like the idea of a laparoscopic repair primarily because of the cosmetic component of that and the ability to fix both the inguinal and the umbilical at the same time. But I would just argue in a woman that with an open inguinal incision, that's really not going to be a visible scar, whereas an umbilical scar will be visible. So that, in my mind, is less of an advantage in this situation. Well, she's going to get an umbilical scar anyway if you're going to fix her umbilical hernia, but I think you need a little more information. Has she had previous surgery? If she's had a lower midline for something else, then it's not worth the struggle that you may have if you're trying to do a laparoscopic repair. I favor a tap approach, especially in women, because I don't want to be in the peritoneal cavity where you risk a... Okay, so you're going to imagine like you're sitting in the doctor's lounge, and I'm the operating surgeon, and I'm going to call you in for some advice, okay? So here's the video. So just to recap, one centimeter umbilical hernia. The first port was done by making an incision directly over the umbilical hernia and putting the first port in under direct vision.

The video shows the insertion of a trocar, revealing the bladder and median umbilical ligament. The surgeon divides the ligament and separates the peritoneum to access the right inguinal plane. A retroperitoneal hematoma is observed, indicating potential vascular injury.

So Melissa, what's your preferred way of getting inside the abdomen for a trocar insertion? Do you use various needles? Do you use the OptiView? Direct vision? Do you do it like this, make a cut? Put it on direct vision. And that's the safest way. What's your preferred way of getting in? So my preferred way for an inguinal, if I'm doing it laparoscopically, is a Hassan cut down with a five millimeter trocar at the umbilicus. I elevate the fascia between coca forceps and size transversely, as Dr. Brunt was talking about. Make sure that I'm all the way through the preperitoneal fat before I do that by sort of, again, lifting up and creating that potential space. If I'm doing a gallbladder, though, I tend to opt to view in at this point. So I do have different methods depending on what surgical technique and truck car positioning I'm using. Ted, what's your preferred way of getting in the abdomen? I mean, you do a lot of obesity surgery as well. So how do you, do you depend on the body habitus or do you do it the same way every time? No, I think you have to tailor it based on the body habitus. And the most terrifying patients are these skinny patients that you can, with one finger, you can push down and occlude their aorta because it's so close to the skin. The other safety issue is be very careful about midline, especially midline from umbilicus cephalad, midline access in a closed fashion. That's the dangerous scenario. If you're off the midline, then a closed technique, varus needle, insufflation, and then first trocar under optic guidance is much safer. You're much less likely to enter a major... retroperitoneal vessel with that approach. And again, in my own practice, if I'm going to do closed initial access, it's a five and not a ten, because you can create potentially damage that is irretrievable with a ten retroperitoneal vessel injury. Okay, so you've answered those questions there, and we're going to get on to the crux of this one here. Okay, so here we are, we're looking at, and you gotta understand, this is like a fast hernia. We didn't really understand what was going on here. The resident was with me, thought that might be liver down there. Like, you really can't assess this information, and then when you realize, you can see a little, then the anesthesiologist tells you, oh, they got a problem, the blood pressure's like, sixty, and she's given her fluids, some penispan, and the pressure's coming up, but it's really hard to kind of come up on the spot with what the heck is going on here, because it's just out of the blue.

And you can see a little puncture wound there. Remember, she had umbilical hernia, skinny woman. And by making the skin incision, I think what we did was we made a tiny laceration of the retroperineum. This is where we're going to need your help, Michael and Melissa and Ted. What are you going to do next? What's your next step? Michael? You're going to have me go first every time here? You're the past president. So actually, before I answer, there's a story I tell our residents. In the early days of Lab Coley, there was a surgeon doing a cut down for an open Hassan insertion put the retractors in, pulled, and was kind of pushing down, and the resident cut with a knife and cut into the iliac artery. So it can happen. And you just have to be really careful. This patient has a large retroperitoneal hematoma, is hypotensive. I think you have to open the abdomen and take care of it. Where you get into really bad problems is with delay, and then the patient starts to kind of cycle. And so I think you have to deal with this immediately. Melissa, anything? I think what's a little different about the institution where I am is we've got a wonderful endovascular group. And I think if this were something that could be treated with an endovascular stent and you had them available in-house right there next door, I don't think that would be a bad option. I one hundred percent agree. If that's going to delay your treatment in any way, you go for the laparotomy and you fix it. Ted, anything to add to that? I would be uncomfortable with basically an expanding retroperitoneal hematoma to just say I'm just going to sort of watch And I think if you have a vascular service that's helpful in your hospital, that's the first step. But I'd be inclined to favor Mike's approach and open right away. Yeah, okay. I can tell you that this actually really was my case. And when this kind of thing's happened, the first thing you think in your mind is that I've killed her. And you're not thinking clearly. You're very likely going to make some cognitive errors in your judgment. And so no matter how much you think you've got the situation under control, you should call for help. And in fact, I did that. I got my colleague Laz Klein to come. He was operating next door. I just comment. I think what you just said is extraordinarily important because your visceral reaction to something like this is, you know, oh, my God, and your heart rate goes up. You start to breathe a little fast. Your mind is racing and thinking about even downstream events. And I think what Dr. Hagan just said is the single most important thing. This is the time. Get somebody else in the room to help with the situation. Yeah. And, you know, Laz came in and he said, oh, this is interesting. Like, totally different approach. It wasn't his problem. And so he said, okay, well, let's get the Buckwalter retractor. It was down in our basement somewhere. We had to dig it up, dust it off somewhere. He says, in the meantime, let me call the vascular surgeon. And we put a little pressure on it. But you look at the hematoma, it's actually quite soft. And so we're watching it to see what's happening. And we can't really kind of do nothing. So we said, well, we might as well just fix her hernia while she's here. We're waiting to see what's happening with the hematoma and for the vascular surgeon to arrive. And it didn't really get any larger. And I'm still not sure whether we did the right thing or not. But we watched it and watched it. And nothing seemed to happen. And it was kind of the vascular surgeon said, well, maybe just keep an eye on it. And she actually did OK.

The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the umbilical hernia, makes a small incision, and dissects into the abdomen under direct vision. The trocar is inserted into the abdomen while pulling the umbilical hernia away from the anterior abdominal wall and the retroperitoneum.

It's pulsating. This is a... I think this is a little different than the one I showed earlier. But it is pulsating and there is the hole there. And the vascular surgeon arrived and did a laparotomy and repaired the right iliac artery. And so the question I have for the panel on this case if they were called in to look at it, is what do you think the outcome would have been if the artery hadn't been repaired, knowing that there was a through and through hole on it? And we'll start with Ted, because he had some concerns about the way the first case was managed. Yeah, I think even if she stabilizes my, you know, she's either going to, develop one, or more likely if she's pretty stable in the OR and you're happy with it, then she'll end up with a pseudoaneurysm in that space. Right. Okay. Melissa? I'm going to be the person who's super gloomy and say that my fear would be the spontaneous rupture. You know, if you're sitting there watching that thing pulsatile in the operating room, I'm not going to be comfortable with her walking out of the hospital twenty-four hours later without knowing it's definitively taken care of. Great. Michael? I agree. I'd be very worried about number one or number two at the least. And I'll just say, I don't think we talk enough about access-related complications because unlike maybe some of the other complications we have, bleeding in the course of an operation, et cetera, they can be fatal for the patient. And a lot of times they're in young patients. And there are Even in laparoscopic cholecystectomy, they're number two on the cause of medical legal suits and payments that are made for that operation. So I think you've got to be very aggressive and proactive about managing these. Great.

And after this case, I talked to two of our vascular surgeons, and I asked them, they said, after looking at that video, there's about a hundred percent chance there's going to be a pseudoaneurysm, um, developed with, with that kind of, uh, image. They suggested that, uh, what Melissa was recommending to go to the, um, endovascular suite would be a reasonable option. Also given that, uh, she's more, she's quite obese, her BMI was thirty-seven, you avoid a laparotomy and all the problems with that. And I think if this happened to me again, or I was involved again, that's what I would suggest. So we have some time for some questions about this. Does anyone in the audience have a question? Hi, Ajay Upadhyay from Oakland, California. One of the options we didn't discuss was to laparoscopically explore these hematomas and do a primary stitch on either the artery or the vein. In the minimally invasive hepatobiliary literature, we routinely suture ligate vessels all the time. And vascular surgeons are more tuned in doing endovascular surgery, they are not into, or open surgery, they are not into minimally invasive approach. So that's another option for all these cases. Yeah, we asked our vascular surgeon if we could do that. I was happy to help him, but he said no. Well, the problem with that is you've got to get proximal and distal control, and you really have to have high-end laparoscopic suturing skills to be able to tackle something like that, and there's You know, there's other branch vessels in that region, and as it turned out, this injury was to not only the anterior side of the artery, but the posterior side, too. It should be very difficult to get to and suture laparoscopically, so I think that would be a challenge, and what you don't want to happen is get into the hematoma, have a lot more bleeding, and now the patient's hypotensive on the table, and you're still got to do a laparotomy to get control. So I think you have to be very, very cautious about approaching that laparoscopy. But there's certainly some areas of bleeding outside of the retroperitoneal vessels where you can do just what you suggested. But I think you have to be careful in that location.

So we'll go over here for another question. The first patient, while you're watching her for two days, did you do a CTA? No. But I think if this happened again, I would do that. That's a very good idea. We really didn't ever determine where the bleeding was from. It turns out, very likely, it wasn't a major vascular injury, because she did okay, but I'm not sure that was the best way to manage it, but that's what we did at the time. Just a follow-up on that. I think I agree you do need the comfort level to do this. minimally invasive repair of vascular injuries. But there is one plus point, when you have a, the pneumoperitoneum does help, unlike in an open case, where blood is going all over, you can't control, you just increase the pressure to about eighteen to twenty, and you have time, put a little pressure on it, with an endo-penet, and if you have a comfort level to repair it, then certainly this could be done. And the pneumoperitoneum does help. And without getting proximal or distal control, these can be repaired. I would disagree with you that pneumoperitoneum will stop major arterial bleeding or even reduce its significance. Nick O'Rourke from Australia. There was a celebrated case in Germany of a ten-year-old boy who was having a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. and the trocar went into the iliac vessel, and the surgeon opened the patient and fixed up the anterior wall. There was a posterior injury, intimal flap, dissection, and the patient lost his leg. So I think you do need vascular input for any of these major vascular injuries, and I think it's crazy to try and tackle it on your own. Okay, thank you very much. I think we'll get on to our next case, and Amy's gonna do it. Can we have this case two put up, please? So the second case is a little bit less dramatic, but certainly has some potential for disaster. Can we go to the beginning of that case? As I get older, I worry when the first slide says call a urologist. I've already given you a little hint of what's about to happen. So we will start. So the scenario is this. Once again, you're hopefully enjoying your coffee in the lounge.

The surgeon performs a sigmoid resection for colorectal cancer, using a medial to lateral approach to dissect the mesentery. The sigmoid colon is elevated, and the mesentery is hanging down as the surgeon begins division using a ligature. During the dissection, a tubular structure is identified, which is the ureter, and it is visibly leaking urine, indicating an injury.

Well, I mean, I think you're safe to always call a urologist if you're not comfortable repairing it. I don't think you should just leave a drain and get out. I think this would probably be amenable to a stent and then repair over it. But I always feel more comfortable, especially for a medical legal standpoint as well, to have that backup for you if you can. Okay. Dr. Brent? Yeah, no, I agree. I'd like to just make a general principle statement, and that is I think that there are certain operations that require for adjacent structures that you don't want to divide or injure a positive identification before you complete your dissection. And I think if you're doing a colectomy, that's one of those operations that you should always have a positive identification of the ureter before you go through and divide the mesentery. Another example would be adrenalectomy, which I do a lot of. Left adrenal, there should always be a positive identification of the pancreas and not just assume that that is the adrenal gland. Because I've seen several cases in which the wrong organ was removed. So I think that's statement number one. Number two. I would definitely call a urologist. This is something that I would probably be comfortable suturing laparoscopically, but I think you're also going to want to stent up that ureter, and that may be something that you don't do. And I think also because of the potential risk of a stricture, because there's likely some additional thermal injury to that ureter, that I would definitely want to have a urologist involved. And I think the other point that's important to make is pre-surgical placement of stents can also help here. So definitely in the difficult diverticulitis patient with a lot of chronic scarring, I get my urology colleagues to put in a stent before I start on that case. And there are a handful of different types. Everybody's got their pros and cons of different ones. We happen to have a lighted stent, sits there and flashes at you, bright colored, very, very helpful laparoscopically where you don't have the haptic feedback of being able to palpate that. Again, hindsight is always twenty twenty, but I think if you knew you were getting into a large colorectal case or you knew you were getting into a difficult diverticular case, that's something to think about. And especially if you're there and you can't identify your anatomy, as Dr. Brunt was saying ahead of time, you can't prove that the ureter is not in your way. That's something you should do before you start dividing that mesentery.

The video shows a laparoscopic procedure where a guide wire is advanced with the help of a laparoscopist, followed by the placement of a stent. The colorectal surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing with the stent in place to repair a significant, but not circumferential, ureteric injury. The final repair is shown with the stent still in place. The surgeon discusses the choice of suture material, preferring a monofilament absorbable suture like PDS for ease of suturing.

The video shows a green arrow indicating the ureter and a red arrow marking the safe zone of dissection above the ureter. The anatomy is clearly identified, contrasting with a previous case where the ureter was tented up with the mesentery, leading to potential injury.

And she's got a problem with recurring cholecystitis and biliary colic over the past six months. And she's been in the emergency room four weeks prior to her gastric bypass because of biliary colic or cholecystitis. And she really wants to have her gallbladder removed at the time of her gastric bypass. So my question to the panel is what advice would you give her Perhaps we'll start with Ted. In a situation like this, like your BMI is sixty, she's got a, you know, she's having recurrent attacks, a fairly colic. What's the best advice for her? So, we used to routinely take The gallbladder is out all the time for every bariatric patient, and then we stopped doing that and looked back and realized that only fourteen percent of those patients actually went on to get a cholecystectomy down the road. I really tailor how sick she feels, how sick she is. If she's had significant biliary colic, multiple trips to the emergency room, and I'm concerned that she has a bad acute cholecystitis, obviously I'm going to deal with the gallbladder alone. If it looks like she's just having some colicky episodes, I feel pretty comfortable taking the gallbladder out at the same time as her bypass. So it really depends how sick she is. Melissa, do you have any thoughts on that? I think in a general statement, I would not hesitate to take out her gallbladder at the same time of doing her bypass. I think the distinction to me would be, can her gallbladder wait until her elective scheduled bypass? If she's been in the ER multiple times and this is becoming a more and more recurrent problem, I'm not going to do her gastric bypass at two in the morning when I'm taking out her gallbladder. in a more urgent setting. That's gonna definitely be a separated and a delayed operation. I think sort of a secondary thing is there is a debate about concomitant procedures, right? I like the idea that if you're taking her to the operating room, you can address both of these at the same time laparoscopically and kind of give her the idea of a two for one, similar to the umbilical and inguinal hernia. I think there are certain things that when you try to put them together, you may compromise your visualization, you may compromise your setup. For example, your port placement for your laparoscopic gastric bypass is not exactly the same place that you would put your ports for a cholecystectomy. I think people need to not be afraid to either add extra ports and put a couple of extra five millimeter trocars on there to make the view what you need, but definitely if you put them together, you can't compromise what you're doing on either independent operation. I think the latter is a really important point. And the only other point that I would make is a laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a patient with a BMI of sixty is a difficult cholecystectomy. Period. It's going to be a difficult case. The exposure is going to be difficult. You may have to do it because the patient's having a lot of symptoms. On the other hand, if their episodes are infrequent, then you may be better off to wait until the patient's lost a fair bit of weight over the next six months and then do the gallbladder then. So I think that's the consideration you have to balance. And it really depends on the individual patient and how symptomatic they are. But this would be a difficult gallbladder. Yeah. I think the important thing is not to do the bypass first and wait with the cholecystectomy if she's having a lot of symptoms. Because when that patient's going through that rapid weight loss period, I guarantee you she's going to be back to the hospital with more complaints. Even with using, we use ActiGol on all our patients post-operatively in that rapid weight loss period. And they still, some of them still end up with symptomatic cholecystectomy that needs surgery. If I can add just one other point. And when they come back, they may have a stone in their bowel duct, and then you can't get to it with an ERCP. Yes, absolutely. And I guess one more comment, if you don't mind. The other thing that we do on a lot of our bariatric patients right before surgery is a liquid diet. It helps shrink the size of their liver, makes it easier. That would be something that if you could tolerate that on her cholecystectomy, to do that for two weeks beforehand, it might make your handling a little bit easier. Yeah, she, in fact, did go on the Optifast for, in her case, probably about three weeks before. And the idea was to make it a little more surgeon friendly.

The surgeon encounters difficulty in identifying structures within the triangle of Calot during a cholecystectomy on a patient with a BMI of sixty. The gallbladder is adherent and inflamed, complicating the dissection. The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to explore the area, attempting to delineate the cystic duct and Hartmann's pouch. Despite efforts, the critical view of safety is not achieved, prompting consideration of a subtotal cholecystectomy as a bailout option.

The surgical team is performing a dissection near the gallbladder, using a laparoscopic Kittner dissector to work the planes bluntly. They are cautious about the proximity to the duodenum, which could indicate the presence of the common bile duct rather than the cystic duct. Despite concerns, they proceed to clip what they believe is the cystic duct. During the procedure, bile leakage is observed from a duct structure in the liver bed, indicating a potential complication.

Melissa, do you want to? Do you want to have a go at this? Absolutely. I am always a fan of more information. So I calangiogram every single person before I cut. That way at least I've got a ductotomy in a common duct if I have misidentified my structure and I can take care of it at that time. I know that the literature says there is no difference in risk of common duct injury based on doing or not doing a calangiogram. My answer to that is I would rather know it's there. So in order to get more information, I would start with a cholangiogram. The other thing that is a great trick if you're not sure about what your anatomy is, most hospitals have those beautiful little twenty five gauge butterfly needles. You can cut the wing portion off that. You can pass that through your five millimeter laparoscopic trocar, put a punctate injury, you know, just from the twenty five gauge into any structure, shoot a cholangiogram through that and hopefully get you more information. But yes, I would start with the cholangiogram. All right. Michael? So I think that the stop point here for me would have been when you see that second ductal structure and you see there's bile coming, that's a stop point. And I would put a catheter in and shoot a cholangiogram then to figure out what the anatomy is rather than proceeding with getting the gallbladder off. Because the risk is, if that's the bile duct, you could then incur a right hepatic artery injury. And if that happens, then you potentially have created a combined biliovascular injury, which outcomes are not as good in there. So that's point number one. Number two, I'll just take a moment to put in a plug for the critical view of safety. There are three elements, two and only two structures, hepatocystic triangles completely cleared of all fat and fibrous tissue, and the lower part of the gallbladder is dissected off the liver bed to expose the lower one-third of the cystic plate. So those are the criteria that we would insist for. Now, to address the second question, so let's say this is a common duct injury. I think the thinking about that, how you should approach that has changed. And the default shouldn't be just to open the patient. And number one, you want to get help at this point. You don't want to deal with this yourself, kind of similar to the retroperitoneal vascular injury approach that we talked about earlier. You want to get an outside set of independent eyes to look at this. And if there's going to be an attempt at repair, it should be done by an experienced HPV surgeon because it's been clearly shown that that will give you the best outcomes. If you don't have that available at your institution, and the patient's not having any major bleeding, it's just spout, then put a drain in and stop and transfer the patient. That's what you should do because that's going to give you the best chance for a good outcome. If you have that expertise in your own institution, then get that surgeon in to help you from this point on. Ted, do you have any comments? I mean, I think I agree with Mike completely. I wasn't comfortable enough with the dissection to clip number one, and I would have done a cholangiogram because it looked like that was a difficult dissection. Anyway, that adds to your reason to do the cholangiogram. I do them routinely, and I think it may not prevent all common duct injuries, but the injury you incur by doing a cholangiogram is a small hole in the duct wall, which is often repairable if you've made your ductotomy in the common duct by accident. You've discovered it, and you can repair it over stent, much more so than taking a big chunk of it out. Okay, so we did a clangogram, as suggested. We went, put the clangogram up into the liver. And the pictures aren't great, but what it does show is that this is the right, it goes into the right lobe of the liver. This is the right, some branch to the right. And distally, you can see the bifurcation to the right and left hepatic ducts and the branch there. So this is... What do you think the injury is? I mean, I'm still not entirely sure, but Michael, Jay? Well, most commonly it's the right posterior hepatic duct branch. I think that's what this likely is in this case. It looks like the common bile duct's intact, and this wouldn't be a duct of lucea, which the true ducts of lucea are really rare. Yeah. Okay, so having this information, I mean, we've kind of run through all this, but in our hospital, we don't have a petability surgeon, but we did call him, and he was at Casa Loma with his kids, but we sent him the video from our smartphones, and the pictures of what was going on.

And he gave us some phone advice. And so what advice do you think he was likely to give Melissa in something like this? Like you do ERCPs, I guess? I do. Yeah. You know, the easiest way to treat a leak in an injury is to get your decompression to head a different direction. So a transgastric ERCP, I would personally do the sphincterotomy component but also place a stent that's been shown to equilibrate the pressure a little bit more than just the sphincterotomy alone. Definitely you've got to address that issue with a higher injury like this, you've got to worry about stricturing. If that happens, a lot of those can be taken care of, but they do require repeat ERCP treatments with stent placement removal dilation, stent placement removal dilation. So I think you either kind of go more definitively if you have the HPV guy who wants to come fix it, or you leave yourself those options for being able to manage it endoscopically with multiple repeat interventions if necessary. But isn't there a loss of substance of the duct in this injury? I mean, that would be my concern about just stenting it. So I think it depends on how much of the right lobe is drained by this duct, but I think you're probably going to have to have that repaired, possibly with a drainage procedure. Ted, do you have any thoughts on what... I was kind of going to say what Mike said is I'm not a hundred percent sure how... much duct is missing, number one, and what segment this is actually draining. Some of those really small posterior branch segments, you know, the teaching has been to just tie that little piece off if it's a small segment, but I can't tell exactly from your cholangiogram how much it is, and I think it's going to need something more than just stenting alone. Okay, so this was our management. We did... and did a trans gastric ERCP. I'll just quickly show you how we did that. We made a gastrotomy and got into the duodenum. We passed a guide wire. He grabbed it with his scope, did a papillotomy, a generous papillotomy. The bladder biliary surgeon wanted to leave the stent as well, but our concern was we'd have to go back and fish it out and thought that might mean another operation. So, you know, we left drains. And just a bit of follow-up here is followed by our surgeon, kidney leak bile, The drain accidentally came out a couple months later and it stopped leaking and she's lost a lot of weight and she's now a huge advocate for bariatric surgery. Can you believe it after all those problems? But I think whenever you run into trouble and you don't have this kind of advice available readily, leaving a drain in is obviously the the key thing, isn't it? And I think a couple of the other points that were brought out, number one, even though the cystic duct was okay, the dissection was not adequate, and it would have been okay to bail out at that point. Number two, When you run into trouble, don't complete the operation. Get more information first before you make it worse. And number three, of course, always ask for help because when you're doing a gallbladder operation and you injure a bile duct, I can tell you the feeling is the same.
